chief_complaint: A 65-year-old male patient presented with chronic dyspnea and a history of trauma to the left hemidiaphragm.
history: The patient had been treated according to COPD management standards without clinical improvement. He had a history of trauma to the left hemidiaphragm and had been experiencing chronic dyspnea. He had been on bronchodilator therapy and oxygen support without any improvement.
exam: Vital signs: BP 120/80 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, RR 24/min. Physical examination revealed decreased lung sounds on the left side.
labs: Ultrasound assessment of diaphragmatic thickness and mobility: decreased mobility of the left hemidiaphragm. Computed Tomography: diaphragmatic palsy. Spirometry: normal lung function.
